''Momotarō no Tanjō'' ({{lang|ja|桃太郎の誕生}})
''Nihon mukashibanashi meii'' ("Japanese Folk Tales") |occupation = {{ill|Esperantist|WD=Q860918}}; {{ill|agronomist|WD=Q1781198}}; {{ill|anthropologist|WD=Q4773904}}; {{ill|ethnologist|WD=Q1371378}}; {{ill|folklorist|WD=Q3075052}}; {{ill|lexicographer|WD=Q14972848}}; {{ill|linguist|WD=Q14467526}}; {{ill|poet|WD=Q49757}}; {{ill|university teacher|WD=Q1622272}}; {{ill|writer|WD=Q36180}} |nationality = {{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} |spouse = Taka Yanagita (1904) |parents = Yakusai Matsuoka (father) Naohei Yanagita (father-in-law) |module = {{Infobox Chinese | child=yes |kyujitai=栁田 國男 |shinjitai=柳田 国男 |romaji=Yanagita Kunio }} |gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} |title= {{ill|Shokikanchō|WD=Q11515193}} |father= {{ill|Misao Matsuoka|WD=Q11530013}} |relatives= {{ill|Eikyū Matsuoka|WD=Q11530016}}; {{ill|Inoue Michiyasu|WD=Q11373249}}; {{ill|Kanae Matsuoka|WD=Q11530083}}; {{ill|Shizuo Matsuoka|WD=Q6278269}} |awards= {{ill|Asahi Prize|WD=Q720434}}; {{ill|Order of Culture|WD=Q530124}}; {{ill|Order of the Rising Sun, 1st class|WD=Q10901470}}; {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|WD=Q2545449}} |notable_works= {{ill|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022}}; {{ill|Kagyū Kō|WD=Q11625819}}; {{ill|Tōno Monogatari|WD=Q10553521}} |native_name= 柳田国男; 柳田國男 |given_name= {{ill|Kunio|WD=Q1635977}} |resting_place= {{ill|Shunjū-en|WD=Q56349527}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kunio Yanagita'''|柳田 國男|Yanagita Kunio|lead=yes|extra=July 31, 1875 – August 8, 1962}} was a Japanese author, scholar, and [[:en:Folklore studies|folklorist]]. He began his career as a bureaucrat, but developed an interest in rural Japan and its folk traditions. This led to a change in his career. His pursuit of this led to his eventual establishment of [[Japanese folklore|Japanese native folkloristics]], or ''minzokugaku'', as an academic field in Japan. As a result, he is often considered to be the father of modern Japanese folklore studies.{{cite book|last1=Mori|first1=Koichi|title=Yanagita Kunio: An Interpretive Study|date=1980|publisher=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|location=Nanzan University|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2210}} ==Early life== Yanagita was born as the fifth child of the Matsuoka family in the town of [[:en:Fukusaki, Hyōgo|Fukusaki]], located in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. He was born with the name Kunio Matsuoka (or Matsuoka Kunio in the Japanese manner of naming), but was adopted into the family of a court justice named Naohei Yanagita. At the time, it was fairly common practice for families without a son [[:en:Japanese adult adoption|to adopt a young boy or man into the family]] to inherit the family’s property. This would often occur through marriage, with the adopting family marrying a daughter of the family off to their chosen heir as a way of binding him to the family. In this particular case, a match was made between the future folklorist and Naohei’s daughter, Taka. The two were wed in 1901, and his name was changed to Kunio Yanagita. Yanagita was known from a fairly young age for his interest in literature, particularly that of poetry. He also was a fan of Western literature. As he began to take an interest in folklore, Yanagita began reading ethnologies by Western anthropologists, such as [[:en:Edward Burnett Tylor|Edward Burnett Tylor]], shaping his later work.[[File:Matsuoka5brothers.JPG|thumb|200px|left|The Matsuoka brothers prior to Kunio's adoption by Yanagita]] ==Career== After graduating with a degree in law from [[:en:Tokyo Imperial University|Tokyo Imperial University]], Yanagita began a career as a civil servant, working for the Department of Agricultural Administration of the [[:en:Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce]], that would last for about 20 years. Over the course of his time in bureaucracy, duties, Yanagita traveled around the countrysides of [[Honshu|Honshū]], the mainland of Japan. During these business trips, Yanagita became increasingly focused on the affairs of rural villages and their agricultural economic policy. As time passed, Yanagita began growing increasingly critical of the lack of concern for local autonomy allowed by the policies favored by his fellow civil servants. He gradually began to advocate in support of these groups, pushing for a shift in agricultural focus to center around cooperatives of small farmers rather than wealthy landlords. It is believed that the pushback he received against his values and ideas may have contributed to his change in careers and shift toward folklore studies.{{Cite journal|last=Oguma|first=Makoto|date=2015|title=The Study of Japan through Japanese Folklore Studies|journal=The Japanese Journal of Psychology|volume=16|issue=92|pages=236–237|doi=10.4992/jjpsy1912.16.236|issn=1884-1066|doi-access=free}} Yanagita’s departure from the Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce allowed him the opportunity to further investigate rural Japan. He began in-depth analysis, traveling around to record stories of local customs, practices, and beliefs. It was at this point that his literary friends, including writer [[:en:Shimazaki Toson|Shimazaki Toson]], began encouraging him to publish works based on oral traditions and customs of rural villages. His most famous example of this is a book known as ''The Legends of Tōno'' (1912). It is a compilation of short stories, practices, beliefs, and anecdotes from [[:en:Tōno, Iwate|Tōno]], a small, rural community surrounded by mountains in [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate]]. From here, Yanagita’s work developed into the anthropological study of folklore that he is still known for today. He published many other works, including several with folklorist [[:en:Kizen Sasaki|Kizen Sasaki]], with whom he collaborated extensively. Yanagita's focus on local traditions was part of a larger effort to insert the lives of commoners into narratives of [[:en:Japanese history|Japanese history]].{{Cite journal|last=Morse|first=Ronald|date=1995|title=Untitled Review of "The Origins of Ethnography in Japan: Yanagita Kunio and His Times"|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2385561|journal=Monumenta Nipponica|volume=50|issue=3|pages=411–413|doi=10.2307/2385561|jstor=2385561|via=JSTOR}} He argued that historical narratives were typically dominated by events pertaining to rulers and high-Ranking officials. Yanagita claimed that these narratives focused on elite-centered historical events and ignored the relative uneventfulness and repetition that characterized the lives of ordinary Japanese people across history. He emphasized the unique practices of different groups of common people, such as ''sanka'' or mountain dwellers, and island dwellers. He also focused primarily on what he saw as the three areas of folklore studies: material objects, oral transmission, and mental or emotional phenomenon. This third Category, accessible only to those who share a deep understanding through similar experiences, is considered the main focus of folklore studies.{{Cite journal|last=Knecht|first=Peter|date=1992|title=Yanagita Kunio and the Folklore Movement: The Search for Japan's National Character and Distinctiveness|url=https://doi.org/10.2307/1178346|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=51|pages=353–355|doi=10.2307/1178346|jstor=1178346|via=JSTOR}} As a whole, Yanagita’s work is highly memorable and genre-defining. He is one of the premiere folklorists of Japan, and he helped to create the field of ''minzokugaku'' itself, earning him the title of “father of modern Japanese folklore.” == Major works == [[File:Kunio yanagita01 1920.jpg|thumb|250px|Yakusai Matsuoka's home]] *''Tōno Monogatari'' (遠野物語) – Yanagita’s most famous work, ''Tōno Monogatari'', is a record of folk legends, stories, and traditions (as opposed to a [[:en:Folklore|folk tale]]) gathered in [[:en:Tōno, Iwate|Tōno]], a city in [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]], Japan. Famous [[:en:Yōkai|Yōkai]] in the stories include [[:en:Kappa (mythical creature)|kappa]] and [[:en:Zashiki-warashi|Zashiki-warashi]].{{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Ronald A. Morse|title=The Legends of Tono|publisher=Lexington Books|year=2008|isbn=978-0-7391-2767-4}} *''Kagyūkō'' (蝸牛考) – Yanagita revealed that the distribution of dialects for the word ''snail'' forms [[:en:Concentric circles|Concentric circles]] on the Japanese archipelago ([[:en:Center versus periphery|Center versus periphery]] theory of dialectical diffusion over time). *''Momotarō no Tanjō'' (桃太郎の誕生) – In this work, Yanagita provides analysis into themes of Japanese folklore and society. The name of the work is derived from the famous Japanese tale of [[Momotarō]], as one of the examples he uses in his commentary on folktales as a form of reference material for understanding Japanese culture. In this work, he analyzes Momotarō to discuss some facets of Japanese society as a whole. His methodology in this has since been followed by many ethnologists and anthropologists. *''Kaijō no Michi'' (海上の道) – This piece, published only a year before Yanagita’s death, records the history, culture, and folk tradition of the [[Okinawa]] islands of Japan. In his studies of Okinawa, Yanagita sought the origins of Japanese culture in the area, though many of his speculations were denied by later researchers. It is also said that his inspiration for this research came from picking up a [[:en:Arecaceae|palm nut]] borne by the [[:en:Kuroshio Current|Kuroshio Current]] when he was wandering on a beach in the cape of [[:en:Cape Irago|Irago Misaki]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]. *''Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale'' – This is a selection of Japanese folktales and data from Yanagita Kunio's ''Nihon mukashi-banashi meii'' (日本昔話名彙), translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer. {{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer|title=Yanagita Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale|publisher=Indiana University Press|year=1986|isbn=0-253-36812-X|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/en/publications/afm/afm9/}} *''Nochi no Kari-kotoba no Ki'' (後狩詞記) – He privately published a work based on his travels around [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]], focusing on the traditions and ways of life of a mountain community from [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], including details about their hunting practices and the vocabulary used to discuss it. This work is considered one of the first works of Japanese folklore studies from Japan. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Agriculture|Ancient Japan|Biography}} * [[:en:Center versus periphery|Center versus periphery]]: Yanagita's theory about [[:en:Dialect|Dialect]]al diffusion and vocabulary propagation over time * [[:en:Nihonjinron|Nihonjinron]] * [[:ja:柳田國男・松岡家顕彰会記念館|Yanagita･Matsuoka Family Memorial (Japanese)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commonscat|Kunio Yanagita}} == External links == * {{Find a Grave|6136353}} {{Japanese folklore long}} {{Suwa Faith}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Kunio Yanagita]] [[arz:كونيو ياناجيتا]] [[bn:কুনিও ইয়ানাগিতা]] [[de:Yanagita Kunio]] [[eo:Yanagita Kunio]] [[es:Kunio Yanagita]] [[fa:کونیو یاناگیتا]] [[fr:Kunio Yanagita]] [[hu:Janagita Kunio]] [[id:Kunio Yanagita]] [[it:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ja:柳田國男]] [[ko:야나기타 구니오]] [[pl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[pt:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ru:Янагита, Кунио]] [[tl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[uk:Янаґіта Куніо]] [[wuu:柳田国男]] [[zh:柳田國男]] {{wikidata link|Q971687}} [[Category:1875 births]] [[Category:1962 deaths]] [[Category:Academic staff of Keio University]] [[Category:Japanese ethnologists]] [[Category:Japanese folklorists]] [[Category:Japanese government officials]] [[Category:Japanese lexicographers]] [[Category:Japanese nationalists]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of Culture]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:University of Tokyo alumni]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

In his studies of Okinawa, Yanagita sought the origins of Japanese culture in the area, though many of his speculations were denied by later researchers. It is also said that his inspiration for this research came from picking up a [[:en:Arecaceae|palm nut]] borne by the [[:en:Kuroshio Current|Kuroshio Current]] when he was wandering on a beach in the cape of [[:en:Cape Irago|Irago Misaki]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]. *''Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale'' – This is a selection of Japanese folktales and data from Yanagita Kunio's ''Nihon mukashi-banashi meii'' (日本昔話名彙), translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer. {{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer|title=Yanagita Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale|publisher=Indiana University Press|year=1986|isbn=0-253-36812-X|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/en/publications/afm/afm9/}} *''Nochi no Kari-kotoba no Ki'' (後狩詞記) – He privately published a work based on his travels around [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]], focusing on the traditions and ways of life of a mountain community from [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], including details about their hunting practices and the vocabulary used to discuss it. This work is considered one of the first works of Japanese folklore studies from Japan. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Agriculture|Ancient Japan|Biography}} * [[:en:Center versus periphery|Center versus periphery]]: Yanagita's theory about [[:en:Dialect|Dialect]]al diffusion and vocabulary propagation over time * [[:en:Nihonjinron|Nihonjinron]] * [[:ja:柳田國男・松岡家顕彰会記念館|Yanagita･Matsuoka Family Memorial (Japanese)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commonscat|Kunio Yanagita}} == External links == * {{Find a Grave|6136353}} {{Japanese folklore long}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[en:Kunio Yanagita]] [[arz:كونيو ياناجيتا]] [[bn:কুনিও ইয়ানাগিতা]] [[de:Yanagita Kunio]] [[eo:Yanagita Kunio]] [[es:Kunio Yanagita]] [[fa:کونیو یاناگیتا]] [[fr:Kunio Yanagita]] [[hu:Janagita Kunio]] [[id:Kunio Yanagita]] [[it:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ja:柳田國男]] [[ko:야나기타 구니오]] [[pl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[pt:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ru:Янагита, Кунио]] [[tl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[uk:Янаґіта Куніо]] [[wuu:柳田国男]] [[zh:柳田國男]] {{wikidata link|Q971687}} [[Category:1875 births]] [[Category:1962 deaths]] [[Category:Academic staff of Keio University]] [[Category:Japanese ethnologists]] [[Category:Japanese folklorists]] [[Category:Japanese government officials]] [[Category:Japanese lexicographers]] [[Category:Japanese nationalists]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of Culture]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:University of Tokyo alumni]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{nobold|{{lang|ja|柳田 國男}}}} |image = File:Kunio Yanagita.jpg |image_size = 200px |caption = Kunio Yanagita, circa 1940 |birth_name = Kunio Matsuoka |birth_date = {{birth date|1875|7|31}} |birth_place = {{ill|Fukusaki|WD=Q1207159}} |death_date = {{death date|1962|8|8}} |death_place = {{ill|Tokyo|WD=Q7473516}} |known_for = Tōno Monogatari ({{lang|ja|遠野物語}})
''Momotarō no Tanjō'' ({{lang|ja|桃太郎の誕生}})
''Nihon mukashibanashi meii'' ("Japanese Folk Tales") |occupation = {{ill|Esperantist|WD=Q860918}}; {{ill|agronomist|WD=Q1781198}}; {{ill|anthropologist|WD=Q4773904}}; {{ill|ethnologist|WD=Q1371378}}; {{ill|folklorist|WD=Q3075052}}; {{ill|lexicographer|WD=Q14972848}}; {{ill|linguist|WD=Q14467526}}; {{ill|poet|WD=Q49757}}; {{ill|university teacher|WD=Q1622272}}; {{ill|writer|WD=Q36180}} |nationality = {{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} |spouse = Taka Yanagita (1904) |parents = Yakusai Matsuoka (father) Naohei Yanagita (father-in-law) |module = {{Infobox Chinese | child=yes |kyujitai=栁田 國男 |shinjitai=柳田 国男 |romaji=Yanagita Kunio }} |gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} |title= {{ill|Shokikanchō|WD=Q11515193}} |father= {{ill|Misao Matsuoka|WD=Q11530013}} |relatives= {{ill|Eikyū Matsuoka|WD=Q11530016}}; {{ill|Inoue Michiyasu|WD=Q11373249}}; {{ill|Kanae Matsuoka|WD=Q11530083}}; {{ill|Shizuo Matsuoka|WD=Q6278269}} |awards= {{ill|Asahi Prize|WD=Q720434}}; {{ill|Order of Culture|WD=Q530124}}; {{ill|Order of the Rising Sun, 1st class|WD=Q10901470}}; {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|WD=Q2545449}} |notable_works= {{ill|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022}}; {{ill|Kagyū Kō|WD=Q11625819}}; {{ill|Tōno Monogatari|WD=Q10553521}} |native_name= 柳田国男; 柳田國男 |given_name= {{ill|Kunio|WD=Q1635977}} |resting_place= {{ill|Shunjū-en|WD=Q56349527}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kunio Yanagita'''|柳田 國男|Yanagita Kunio|lead=yes|extra=July 31, 1875 – August 8, 1962}} was a Japanese author, scholar, and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklorist}}. He began his career as a bureaucrat, but developed an interest in rural Japan and its folk traditions. This led to a change in his career. His pursuit of this led to his eventual establishment of [[Japanese folklore|Japanese native folkloristics]], or ''minzokugaku'', as an academic field in Japan. As a result, he is often considered to be the father of modern Japanese folklore studies.{{cite book|last1=Mori|first1=Koichi|title=Yanagita Kunio: An Interpretive Study|date=1980|publisher=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|location=Nanzan University|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2210}} ==Early life== Yanagita was born as the fifth child of the Matsuoka family in the town of {{ill|Fukusaki, Hyōgo|en|Fukusaki, Hyōgo|WD=Q1207159|lt=Fukusaki}}, located in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. He was born with the name Kunio Matsuoka (or Matsuoka Kunio in the Japanese manner of naming), but was adopted into the family of a court justice named Naohei Yanagita. At the time, it was fairly common practice for families without a son {{ill|Japanese adult adoption|en|Japanese adult adoption|WD=Q17050042|lt=to adopt a young boy or man into the family}} to inherit the family’s property. This would often occur through marriage, with the adopting family marrying a daughter of the family off to their chosen heir as a way of binding him to the family. In this particular case, a match was made between the future folklorist and Naohei’s daughter, Taka. The two were wed in 1901, and his name was changed to Kunio Yanagita. Yanagita was known from a fairly young age for his interest in literature, particularly that of poetry. He also was a fan of Western literature. As he began to take an interest in folklore, Yanagita began reading ethnologies by Western anthropologists, such as {{ill|Edward Burnett Tylor|en|Edward Burnett Tylor|WD=Q141037|lt=Edward Burnett Tylor}}, shaping his later work.[[File:Matsuoka5brothers.JPG|thumb|200px|left|The Matsuoka brothers prior to Kunio's adoption by Yanagita]] ==Career== After graduating with a degree in law from [[:en:Tokyo Imperial University|Tokyo Imperial University]], Yanagita began a career as a civil servant, working for the Department of Agricultural Administration of the {{ill|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|en|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|WD=Q3315480|lt=Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce}}, that would last for about 20 years. Over the course of his time in bureaucracy, duties, Yanagita traveled around the countrysides of [[Honshu|Honshū]], the mainland of Japan. During these business trips, Yanagita became increasingly focused on the affairs of rural villages and their agricultural economic policy. As time passed, Yanagita began growing increasingly critical of the lack of concern for local autonomy allowed by the policies favored by his fellow civil servants. He gradually began to advocate in support of these groups, pushing for a shift in agricultural focus to center around cooperatives of small farmers rather than wealthy landlords. It is believed that the pushback he received against his values and ideas may have contributed to his change in careers and shift toward folklore studies.{{Cite journal|last=Oguma|first=Makoto|date=2015|title=The Study of Japan through Japanese Folklore Studies|journal=The Japanese Journal of Psychology|volume=16|issue=92|pages=236–237|doi=10.4992/jjpsy1912.16.236|issn=1884-1066|doi-access=free}} Yanagita’s departure from the Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce allowed him the opportunity to further investigate rural Japan. He began in-depth analysis, traveling around to record stories of local customs, practices, and beliefs. It was at this point that his literary friends, including writer [[:en:Shimazaki Toson|Shimazaki Toson]], began encouraging him to publish works based on oral traditions and customs of rural villages. His most famous example of this is a book known as ''The Legends of Tōno'' (1912). It is a compilation of short stories, practices, beliefs, and anecdotes from {{ill|Tōno, Iwate|en|Tōno, Iwate|WD=Q646766|lt=Tōno}}, a small, rural community surrounded by mountains in {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate}}. From here, Yanagita’s work developed into the anthropological study of folklore that he is still known for today. He published many other works, including several with folklorist {{ill|Kizen Sasaki|en|Kizen Sasaki|WD=Q2646063|lt=Kizen Sasaki}}, with whom he collaborated extensively. Yanagita's focus on local traditions was part of a larger effort to insert the lives of commoners into narratives of [[:en:Japanese history|Japanese history]].{{Cite journal|last=Morse|first=Ronald|date=1995|title=Untitled Review of "The Origins of Ethnography in Japan: Yanagita Kunio and His Times"|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2385561|journal=Monumenta Nipponica|volume=50|issue=3|pages=411–413|doi=10.2307/2385561|jstor=2385561|via=JSTOR}} He argued that historical narratives were typically dominated by events pertaining to rulers and high-Ranking officials. Yanagita claimed that these narratives focused on elite-centered historical events and ignored the relative uneventfulness and repetition that characterized the lives of ordinary Japanese people across history. He emphasized the unique practices of different groups of common people, such as ''sanka'' or mountain dwellers, and island dwellers. He also focused primarily on what he saw as the three areas of folklore studies: material objects, oral transmission, and mental or emotional phenomenon. This third Category, accessible only to those who share a deep understanding through similar experiences, is considered the main focus of folklore studies.{{Cite journal|last=Knecht|first=Peter|date=1992|title=Yanagita Kunio and the Folklore Movement: The Search for Japan's National Character and Distinctiveness|url=https://doi.org/10.2307/1178346|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=51|pages=353–355|doi=10.2307/1178346|jstor=1178346|via=JSTOR}} As a whole, Yanagita’s work is highly memorable and genre-defining. He is one of the premiere folklorists of Japan, and he helped to create the field of ''minzokugaku'' itself, earning him the title of “father of modern Japanese folklore.” == Major works == [[File:Kunio yanagita01 1920.jpg|thumb|250px|Yakusai Matsuoka's home]] *''Tōno Monogatari'' (遠野物語) – Yanagita’s most famous work, ''Tōno Monogatari'', is a record of folk legends, stories, and traditions (as opposed to a {{ill|Folklore|en|Folklore|WD=Q36192|lt=folk tale}}) gathered in {{ill|Tōno, Iwate|en|Tōno, Iwate|WD=Q646766|lt=Tōno}}, a city in {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, Japan. Famous {{ill|Yōkai|en|Yōkai|WD=Q503186|lt=Yōkai}} in the stories include [[:en:Kappa (mythical creature)|kappa]] and {{ill|Zashiki-warashi|en|Zashiki-warashi|WD=Q148209|lt=Zashiki-warashi}}.{{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Ronald A. Morse|title=The Legends of Tono|publisher=Lexington Books|year=2008|isbn=978-0-7391-2767-4}} *''Kagyūkō'' (蝸牛考) – Yanagita revealed that the distribution of dialects for the word ''snail'' forms {{ill|Concentric circles|en|Concentric circles|WD=Q67251689|lt=Concentric circles}} on the Japanese archipelago ({{ill|Center versus periphery|en|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022|lt=Center versus periphery}} theory of dialectical diffusion over time). *''Momotarō no Tanjō'' (桃太郎の誕生) – In this work, Yanagita provides analysis into themes of Japanese folklore and society. The name of the work is derived from the famous Japanese tale of [[Momotarō]], as one of the examples he uses in his commentary on folktales as a form of reference material for understanding Japanese culture. In this work, he analyzes Momotarō to discuss some facets of Japanese society as a whole. His methodology in this has since been followed by many ethnologists and anthropologists. *''Kaijō no Michi'' (海上の道) – This piece, published only a year before Yanagita’s death, records the history, culture, and folk tradition of the [[Okinawa]] islands of Japan. In his studies of Okinawa, Yanagita sought the origins of Japanese culture in the area, though many of his speculations were denied by later researchers. It is also said that his inspiration for this research came from picking up a {{ill|Arecaceae|en|Arecaceae|WD=Q14080|lt=palm nut}} borne by the {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}} when he was wandering on a beach in the cape of {{ill|Cape Irago|en|Cape Irago|WD=Q371114|lt=Irago Misaki}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. *''Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale'' – This is a selection of Japanese folktales and data from Yanagita Kunio's ''Nihon mukashi-banashi meii'' (日本昔話名彙), translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer. {{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer|title=Yanagita Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale|publisher=Indiana University Press|year=1986|isbn=0-253-36812-X|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/en/publications/afm/afm9/}} *''Nochi no Kari-kotoba no Ki'' (後狩詞記) – He privately published a work based on his travels around [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]], focusing on the traditions and ways of life of a mountain community from {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, including details about their hunting practices and the vocabulary used to discuss it. This work is considered one of the first works of Japanese folklore studies from Japan. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Agriculture|Ancient Japan|Biography}} * {{ill|Center versus periphery|en|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022|lt=Center versus periphery}}: Yanagita's theory about {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect}}al diffusion and vocabulary propagation over time * {{ill|Nihonjinron|en|Nihonjinron|WD=Q1761112|lt=Nihonjinron}} * [[:ja:柳田國男・松岡家顕彰会記念館|Yanagita･Matsuoka Family Memorial (Japanese)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commonscat|Kunio Yanagita}} == External links == * {{Find a Grave|6136353}} {{Japanese folklore long}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[en:Kunio Yanagita]] [[arz:كونيو ياناجيتا]] [[bn:কুনিও ইয়ানাগিতা]] [[de:Yanagita Kunio]] [[eo:Yanagita Kunio]] [[es:Kunio Yanagita]] [[fa:کونیو یاناگیتا]] [[fr:Kunio Yanagita]] [[hu:Janagita Kunio]] [[id:Kunio Yanagita]] [[it:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ja:柳田國男]] [[ko:야나기타 구니오]] [[pl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[pt:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ru:Янагита, Кунио]] [[tl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[uk:Янаґіта Куніо]] [[wuu:柳田国男]] [[zh:柳田國男]] {{wikidata link|Q971687}} [[Category:1875 births]] [[Category:1962 deaths]] [[Category:Academic staff of Keio University]] [[Category:Japanese ethnologists]] [[Category:Japanese folklorists]] [[Category:Japanese government officials]] [[Category:Japanese lexicographers]] [[Category:Japanese nationalists]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of Culture]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:University of Tokyo alumni]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

He began his career as a bureaucrat, but developed an interest in rural Japan and its folk traditions. This led to a change in his career. His pursuit of this led to his eventual establishment of [[Japanese folklore|Japanese native folkloristics]], or ''minzokugaku'', as an academic field in Japan. As a result, he is often considered to be the father of modern Japanese folklore studies.{{cite book|last1=Mori|first1=Koichi|title=Yanagita Kunio: An Interpretive Study|date=1980|publisher=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|location=Nanzan University|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2210}} ==Early life== Yanagita was born as the fifth child of the Matsuoka family in the town of {{ill|Fukusaki, Hyōgo|en|Fukusaki, Hyōgo|WD=Q1207159|lt=Fukusaki}}, located in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. He was born with the name Kunio Matsuoka (or Matsuoka Kunio in the Japanese manner of naming), but was adopted into the family of a court justice named Naohei Yanagita. At the time, it was fairly common practice for families without a son {{ill|Japanese adult adoption|en|Japanese adult adoption|WD=Q17050042|lt=to adopt a young boy or man into the family}} to inherit the family’s property. This would often occur through marriage, with the adopting family marrying a daughter of the family off to their chosen heir as a way of binding him to the family. In this particular case, a match was made between the future folklorist and Naohei’s daughter, Taka. The two were wed in 1901, and his name was changed to Kunio Yanagita. Yanagita was known from a fairly young age for his interest in literature, particularly that of poetry. He also was a fan of Western literature. As he began to take an interest in folklore, Yanagita began reading ethnologies by Western anthropologists, such as {{ill|Edward Burnett Tylor|en|Edward Burnett Tylor|WD=Q141037|lt=Edward Burnett Tylor}}, shaping his later work.[[File:Matsuoka5brothers.JPG|thumb|200px|left|The Matsuoka brothers prior to Kunio's adoption by Yanagita]] ==Career== After graduating with a degree in law from [[:en:Tokyo Imperial University|Tokyo Imperial University]], Yanagita began a career as a civil servant, working for the Department of Agricultural Administration of the {{ill|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|en|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|WD=Q3315480|lt=Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce}}, that would last for about 20 years. Over the course of his time in bureaucracy, duties, Yanagita traveled around the countrysides of [[Honshu|Honshū]], the mainland of Japan. During these business trips, Yanagita became increasingly focused on the affairs of rural villages and their agricultural economic policy. As time passed, Yanagita began growing increasingly critical of the lack of concern for local autonomy allowed by the policies favored by his fellow civil servants. He gradually began to advocate in support of these groups, pushing for a shift in agricultural focus to center around cooperatives of small farmers rather than wealthy landlords. It is believed that the pushback he received against his values and ideas may have contributed to his change in careers and shift toward folklore studies.{{Cite journal|last=Oguma|first=Makoto|date=2015|title=The Study of Japan through Japanese Folklore Studies|journal=The Japanese Journal of Psychology|volume=16|issue=92|pages=236–237|doi=10.4992/jjpsy1912.16.236|issn=1884-1066|doi-access=free}} Yanagita’s departure from the Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce allowed him the opportunity to further investigate rural Japan. He began in-depth analysis, traveling around to record stories of local customs, practices, and beliefs. It was at this point that his literary friends, including writer [[:en:Shimazaki Toson|Shimazaki Toson]], began encouraging him to publish works based on oral traditions and customs of rural villages. His most famous example of this is a book known as ''The Legends of Tōno'' (1912). It is a compilation of short stories, practices, beliefs, and anecdotes from {{ill|Tōno, Iwate|en|Tōno, Iwate|WD=Q646766|lt=Tōno}}, a small, rural community surrounded by mountains in {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate}}. From here, Yanagita’s work developed into the anthropological study of folklore that he is still known for today. He published many other works, including several with folklorist {{ill|Kizen Sasaki|en|Kizen Sasaki|WD=Q2646063|lt=Kizen Sasaki}}, with whom he collaborated extensively. Yanagita's focus on local traditions was part of a larger effort to insert the lives of commoners into narratives of [[:en:Japanese history|Japanese history]].{{Cite journal|last=Morse|first=Ronald|date=1995|title=Untitled Review of "The Origins of Ethnography in Japan: Yanagita Kunio and His Times"|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2385561|journal=Monumenta Nipponica|volume=50|issue=3|pages=411–413|doi=10.2307/2385561|jstor=2385561|via=JSTOR}} He argued that historical narratives were typically dominated by events pertaining to rulers and high-Ranking officials. Yanagita claimed that these narratives focused on elite-centered historical events and ignored the relative uneventfulness and repetition that characterized the lives of ordinary Japanese people across history. He emphasized the unique practices of different groups of common people, such as ''sanka'' or mountain dwellers, and island dwellers. He also focused primarily on what he saw as the three areas of folklore studies: material objects, oral transmission, and mental or emotional phenomenon. This third Category, accessible only to those who share a deep understanding through similar experiences, is considered the main focus of folklore studies.{{Cite journal|last=Knecht|first=Peter|date=1992|title=Yanagita Kunio and the Folklore Movement: The Search for Japan's National Character and Distinctiveness|url=https://doi.org/10.2307/1178346|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=51|pages=353–355|doi=10.2307/1178346|jstor=1178346|via=JSTOR}} As a whole, Yanagita’s work is highly memorable and genre-defining. He is one of the premiere folklorists of Japan, and he helped to create the field of ''minzokugaku'' itself, earning him the title of “father of modern Japanese folklore.” == Major works == [[File:Kunio yanagita01 1920.jpg|thumb|250px|Yakusai Matsuoka's home]] *''Tōno Monogatari'' (遠野物語) – Yanagita’s most famous work, ''Tōno Monogatari'', is a record of folk legends, stories, and traditions (as opposed to a {{ill|Folklore|en|Folklore|WD=Q36192|lt=folk tale}}) gathered in {{ill|Tōno, Iwate|en|Tōno, Iwate|WD=Q646766|lt=Tōno}}, a city in {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, Japan. Famous {{ill|Yōkai|en|Yōkai|WD=Q503186|lt=Yōkai}} in the stories include [[:en:Kappa (mythical creature)|kappa]] and {{ill|Zashiki-warashi|en|Zashiki-warashi|WD=Q148209|lt=Zashiki-warashi}}.{{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Ronald A. Morse|title=The Legends of Tono|publisher=Lexington Books|year=2008|isbn=978-0-7391-2767-4}} *''Kagyūkō'' (蝸牛考) – Yanagita revealed that the distribution of dialects for the word ''snail'' forms {{ill|Concentric circles|en|Concentric circles|WD=Q67251689|lt=Concentric circles}} on the Japanese archipelago ({{ill|Center versus periphery|en|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022|lt=Center versus periphery}} theory of dialectical diffusion over time). *''Momotarō no Tanjō'' (桃太郎の誕生) – In this work, Yanagita provides analysis into themes of Japanese folklore and society. The name of the work is derived from the famous Japanese tale of [[Momotarō]], as one of the examples he uses in his commentary on folktales as a form of reference material for understanding Japanese culture. In this work, he analyzes Momotarō to discuss some facets of Japanese society as a whole. His methodology in this has since been followed by many ethnologists and anthropologists. *''Kaijō no Michi'' (海上の道) – This piece, published only a year before Yanagita’s death, records the history, culture, and folk tradition of the [[Okinawa]] islands of Japan. In his studies of Okinawa, Yanagita sought the origins of Japanese culture in the area, though many of his speculations were denied by later researchers. It is also said that his inspiration for this research came from picking up a {{ill|Arecaceae|en|Arecaceae|WD=Q14080|lt=palm nut}} borne by the {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}} when he was wandering on a beach in the cape of {{ill|Cape Irago|en|Cape Irago|WD=Q371114|lt=Irago Misaki}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. *''Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale'' – This is a selection of Japanese folktales and data from Yanagita Kunio's ''Nihon mukashi-banashi meii'' (日本昔話名彙), translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer. {{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer|title=Yanagita Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale|publisher=Indiana University Press|year=1986|isbn=0-253-36812-X|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/en/publications/afm/afm9/}} *''Nochi no Kari-kotoba no Ki'' (後狩詞記) – He privately published a work based on his travels around [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]], focusing on the traditions and ways of life of a mountain community from {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, including details about their hunting practices and the vocabulary used to discuss it. This work is considered one of the first works of Japanese folklore studies from Japan. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Agriculture|Ancient Japan|Biography}} * {{ill|Center versus periphery|en|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022|lt=Center versus periphery}}: Yanagita's theory about {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect}}al diffusion and vocabulary propagation over time * {{ill|Nihonjinron|en|Nihonjinron|WD=Q1761112|lt=Nihonjinron}} * [[:ja:柳田國男・松岡家顕彰会記念館|Yanagita･Matsuoka Family Memorial (Japanese)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commonscat|Kunio Yanagita}} == External links == * {{Find a Grave|6136353}} {{Japanese folklore long}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[en:Kunio Yanagita]] [[arz:كونيو ياناجيتا]] [[bn:কুনিও ইয়ানাগিতা]] [[de:Yanagita Kunio]] [[eo:Yanagita Kunio]] [[es:Kunio Yanagita]] [[fa:کونیو یاناگیتا]] [[fr:Kunio Yanagita]] [[hu:Janagita Kunio]] [[id:Kunio Yanagita]] [[it:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ja:柳田國男]] [[ko:야나기타 구니오]] [[pl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[pt:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ru:Янагита, Кунио]] [[tl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[uk:Янаґіта Куніо]] [[wuu:柳田国男]] [[zh:柳田國男]] {{wikidata link|Q971687}} [[Category:1875 births]] [[Category:1962 deaths]] [[Category:Academic staff of Keio University]] [[Category:Japanese ethnologists]] [[Category:Japanese folklorists]] [[Category:Japanese government officials]] [[Category:Japanese lexicographers]] [[Category:Japanese nationalists]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of Culture]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:University of Tokyo alumni]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

He began his career as a bureaucrat, but developed an interest in rural Japan and its folk traditions. This led to a change in his career. His pursuit of this led to his eventual establishment of [[Japanese folklore|Japanese native folkloristics]], or ''minzokugaku'', as an academic field in Japan. This would often occur through marriage, with the adopting family marrying a daughter of the family off to their chosen heir as a way of binding him to the family. During these business trips, Yanagita became increasingly focused on the affairs of rural villages and their agricultural economic policy. In his studies of Okinawa, Yanagita sought the origins of Japanese culture in the area, though many of his speculations were denied by later researchers. It is also said that his inspiration for this research came from picking up a {{ill|Arecaceae|en|Arecaceae|WD=Q14080|lt=palm nut}} borne by the {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}} when he was wandering on a beach in the cape of {{ill|Cape Irago|en|Cape Irago|WD=Q371114|lt=Irago Misaki}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. *''Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale'' – This is a selection of Japanese folktales and data from Yanagita Kunio's ''Nihon mukashi-banashi meii'' (日本昔話名彙), translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer. {{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer|title=Yanagita Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale|publisher=Indiana University Press|year=1986|isbn=0-253-36812-X|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/en/publications/afm/afm9/}} *''Nochi no Kari-kotoba no Ki'' (後狩詞記) – He privately published a work based on his travels around [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]], focusing on the traditions and ways of life of a mountain community from {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, including details about their hunting practices and the vocabulary used to discuss it. This work is considered one of the first works of Japanese folklore studies from Japan. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Agriculture|Ancient Japan|Biography}} * {{ill|Center versus periphery|en|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022|lt=Center versus periphery}}: Yanagita's theory about {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect}}al diffusion and vocabulary propagation over time * {{ill|Nihonjinron|en|Nihonjinron|WD=Q1761112|lt=Nihonjinron}} * [[:ja:柳田國男・松岡家顕彰会記念館|Yanagita･Matsuoka Family Memorial (Japanese)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commonscat|Kunio Yanagita}} == External links == * {{Find a Grave|6136353}} {{Japanese folklore long}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[en:Kunio Yanagita]] [[arz:كونيو ياناجيتا]] [[bn:কুনিও ইয়ানাগিতা]] [[de:Yanagita Kunio]] [[eo:Yanagita Kunio]] [[es:Kunio Yanagita]] [[fa:کونیو یاناگیتا]] [[fr:Kunio Yanagita]] [[hu:Janagita Kunio]] [[id:Kunio Yanagita]] [[it:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ja:柳田國男]] [[ko:야나기타 구니오]] [[pl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[pt:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ru:Янагита, Кунио]] [[tl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[uk:Янаґіта Куніо]] [[wuu:柳田国男]] [[zh:柳田國男]] {{wikidata link|Q971687}} [[Category:1875 births]] [[Category:1962 deaths]] [[Category:Academic staff of Keio University]] [[Category:Japanese ethnologists]] [[Category:Japanese folklorists]] [[Category:Japanese government officials]] [[Category:Japanese lexicographers]] [[Category:Japanese nationalists]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of Culture]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:University of Tokyo alumni]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[arz:كونيو ياناجيتا]][[bn:কুনিও ইয়ানাগিতা]][[de:Yanagita Kunio]][[en:Kunio Yanagita]][[eo:Yanagita Kunio]][[es:Kunio Yanagita]][[fa:کونیو یاناگیتا]][[fr:Kunio Yanagita]][[hu:Janagita Kunio]][[id:Kunio Yanagita]][[it:Kunio Yanagita]][[ja:柳田國男]][[ko:야나기타 구니오]][[pl:Kunio Yanagita]][[pt:Kunio Yanagita]][[ru:Янагита, Кунио]][[tl:Kunio Yanagita]][[uk:Янаґіта Куніо]][[wuu:柳田国男]][[zh:柳田國男]] [[arz:كونيو ياناجيتا]][[bn:কুনিও ইয়ানাগিতা]][[de:Yanagita Kunio]][[en:Kunio Yanagita]][[eo:Yanagita Kunio]][[es:Kunio Yanagita]][[fa:کونیو یاناگیتا]][[fr:Kunio Yanagita]][[hu:Janagita Kunio]][[id:Kunio Yanagita]][[it:Kunio Yanagita]][[ja:柳田國男]][[ko:야나기타 구니오]][[pl:Kunio Yanagita]][[pt:Kunio Yanagita]][[ru:Янагита, Кунио]][[tl:Kunio Yanagita]][[uk:Янаґіта Куніо]][[wuu:柳田国男]][[zh:柳田國男]] [[arz:كونيو ياناجيتا]][[bn:কুনিও ইয়ানাগিতা]][[de:Yanagita Kunio]][[en:Kunio Yanagita]][[eo:Yanagita Kunio]][[es:Kunio Yanagita]][[fa:کونیو یاناگیتا]][[fr:Kunio Yanagita]][[hu:Janagita Kunio]][[id:Kunio Yanagita]][[it:Kunio Yanagita]][[ja:柳田國男]][[ko:야나기타 구니오]][[pl:Kunio Yanagita]][[pt:Kunio Yanagita]][[ru:Янагита, Кунио]][[tl:Kunio Yanagita]][[uk:Янаґіта Куніо]][[wuu:柳田国男]][[zh:柳田國男]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q971687]] Wikidata name: Kunio Yanagita [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese folklorist}} {{Infobox person |name = Kunio Yanagita
{{nobold|{{lang|ja|柳田 國男}}}} |image = File:Kunio Yanagita.jpg |image_size = 200px |caption = Kunio Yanagita, circa 1940 |birth_name = Kunio Matsuoka |birth_date = {{birth date|1875|7|31}} |birth_place = {{ill|Fukusaki|WD=Q1207159}} |death_date = {{death date|1962|8|8}} |death_place = {{ill|Tokyo|WD=Q7473516}} |known_for = Tōno Monogatari ({{lang|ja|遠野物語}})
''Momotarō no Tanjō'' ({{lang|ja|桃太郎の誕生}})
''Nihon mukashibanashi meii'' ("Japanese Folk Tales") |occupation = {{ill|Esperantist|WD=Q860918}}; {{ill|agronomist|WD=Q1781198}}; {{ill|anthropologist|WD=Q4773904}}; {{ill|ethnologist|WD=Q1371378}}; {{ill|folklorist|WD=Q3075052}}; {{ill|lexicographer|WD=Q14972848}}; {{ill|linguist|WD=Q14467526}}; {{ill|poet|WD=Q49757}}; {{ill|university teacher|WD=Q1622272}}; {{ill|writer|WD=Q36180}} |nationality = {{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} |spouse = Taka Yanagita (1904) |parents = Yakusai Matsuoka (father) Naohei Yanagita (father-in-law) |module = {{Infobox Chinese | child=yes |kyujitai=栁田 國男 |shinjitai=柳田 国男 |romaji=Yanagita Kunio }} |gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} |title= {{ill|Shokikanchō|WD=Q11515193}} |father= {{ill|Misao Matsuoka|WD=Q11530013}} |relatives= {{ill|Eikyū Matsuoka|WD=Q11530016}}; {{ill|Inoue Michiyasu|WD=Q11373249}}; {{ill|Kanae Matsuoka|WD=Q11530083}}; {{ill|Shizuo Matsuoka|WD=Q6278269}} |awards= {{ill|Asahi Prize|WD=Q720434}}; {{ill|Order of Culture|WD=Q530124}}; {{ill|Order of the Rising Sun, 1st class|WD=Q10901470}}; {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|WD=Q2545449}} |notable_works= {{ill|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022}}; {{ill|Kagyū Kō|WD=Q11625819}}; {{ill|Tōno Monogatari|WD=Q10553521}} |native_name= 柳田国男; 柳田國男 |given_name= {{ill|Kunio|WD=Q1635977}} |resting_place= {{ill|Shunjū-en|WD=Q56349527}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kunio Yanagita'''|柳田 國男|Yanagita Kunio|lead=yes|extra=July 31, 1875 – August 8, 1962}} was a Japanese author, scholar, and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklorist}}. He began his career as a bureaucrat, but developed an interest in rural Japan and its folk traditions. This led to a change in his career. His pursuit of this led to his eventual establishment of [[Japanese folklore|Japanese native folkloristics]], or ''minzokugaku'', as an academic field in Japan. As a result, he is often considered to be the father of modern Japanese folklore studies.{{cite book|last1=Mori|first1=Koichi|title=Yanagita Kunio: An Interpretive Study|date=1980|publisher=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|location=Nanzan University|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2210}} ==Early life== Yanagita was born as the fifth child of the Matsuoka family in the town of {{ill|Fukusaki, Hyōgo|en|Fukusaki, Hyōgo|WD=Q1207159|lt=Fukusaki}}, located in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. He was born with the name Kunio Matsuoka (or Matsuoka Kunio in the Japanese manner of naming), but was adopted into the family of a court justice named Naohei Yanagita. At the time, it was fairly common practice for families without a son {{ill|Japanese adult adoption|en|Japanese adult adoption|WD=Q17050042|lt=to adopt a young boy or man into the family}} to inherit the family’s property. This would often occur through marriage, with the adopting family marrying a daughter of the family off to their chosen heir as a way of binding him to the family. In this particular case, a match was made between the future folklorist and Naohei’s daughter, Taka. The two were wed in 1901, and his name was changed to Kunio Yanagita. Yanagita was known from a fairly young age for his interest in literature, particularly that of poetry. He also was a fan of Western literature. As he began to take an interest in folklore, Yanagita began reading ethnologies by Western anthropologists, such as {{ill|Edward Burnett Tylor|en|Edward Burnett Tylor|WD=Q141037|lt=Edward Burnett Tylor}}, shaping his later work.[[File:Matsuoka5brothers.JPG|thumb|200px|left|The Matsuoka brothers prior to Kunio's adoption by Yanagita]] ==Career== After graduating with a degree in law from [[:en:Tokyo Imperial University|Tokyo Imperial University]], Yanagita began a career as a civil servant, working for the Department of Agricultural Administration of the {{ill|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|en|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|WD=Q3315480|lt=Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce}}, that would last for about 20 years. Over the course of his time in bureaucracy, duties, Yanagita traveled around the countrysides of [[Honshu|Honshū]], the mainland of Japan. During these business trips, Yanagita became increasingly focused on the affairs of rural villages and their agricultural economic policy. As time passed, Yanagita began growing increasingly critical of the lack of concern for local autonomy allowed by the policies favored by his fellow civil servants. He gradually began to advocate in support of these groups, pushing for a shift in agricultural focus to center around cooperatives of small farmers rather than wealthy landlords. It is believed that the pushback he received against his values and ideas may have contributed to his change in careers and shift toward folklore studies.{{Cite journal|last=Oguma|first=Makoto|date=2015|title=The Study of Japan through Japanese Folklore Studies|journal=The Japanese Journal of Psychology|volume=16|issue=92|pages=236–237|doi=10.4992/jjpsy1912.16.236|issn=1884-1066|doi-access=free}} Yanagita’s departure from the Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce allowed him the opportunity to further investigate rural Japan. He began in-depth analysis, traveling around to record stories of local customs, practices, and beliefs. It was at this point that his literary friends, including writer [[:en:Shimazaki Toson|Shimazaki Toson]], began encouraging him to publish works based on oral traditions and customs of rural villages. His most famous example of this is a book known as ''The Legends of Tōno'' (1912). It is a compilation of short stories, practices, beliefs, and anecdotes from {{ill|Tōno, Iwate|en|Tōno, Iwate|WD=Q646766|lt=Tōno}}, a small, rural community surrounded by mountains in {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate}}. From here, Yanagita’s work developed into the anthropological study of folklore that he is still known for today. He published many other works, including several with folklorist {{ill|Kizen Sasaki|en|Kizen Sasaki|WD=Q2646063|lt=Kizen Sasaki}}, with whom he collaborated extensively. Yanagita's focus on local traditions was part of a larger effort to insert the lives of commoners into narratives of [[:en:Japanese history|Japanese history]].{{Cite journal|last=Morse|first=Ronald|date=1995|title=Untitled Review of "The Origins of Ethnography in Japan: Yanagita Kunio and His Times"|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2385561|journal=Monumenta Nipponica|volume=50|issue=3|pages=411–413|doi=10.2307/2385561|jstor=2385561|via=JSTOR}} He argued that historical narratives were typically dominated by events pertaining to rulers and high-Ranking officials. Yanagita claimed that these narratives focused on elite-centered historical events and ignored the relative uneventfulness and repetition that characterized the lives of ordinary Japanese people across history. He emphasized the unique practices of different groups of common people, such as ''sanka'' or mountain dwellers, and island dwellers. He also focused primarily on what he saw as the three areas of folklore studies: material objects, oral transmission, and mental or emotional phenomenon. This third Category, accessible only to those who share a deep understanding through similar experiences, is considered the main focus of folklore studies.{{Cite journal|last=Knecht|first=Peter|date=1992|title=Yanagita Kunio and the Folklore Movement: The Search for Japan's National Character and Distinctiveness|url=https://doi.org/10.2307/1178346|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=51|pages=353–355|doi=10.2307/1178346|jstor=1178346|via=JSTOR}} As a whole, Yanagita’s work is highly memorable and genre-defining. He is one of the premiere folklorists of Japan, and he helped to create the field of ''minzokugaku'' itself, earning him the title of “father of modern Japanese folklore.” == Major works == [[File:Kunio yanagita01 1920.jpg|thumb|250px|Yakusai Matsuoka's home]] *''Tōno Monogatari'' (遠野物語) – Yanagita’s most famous work, ''Tōno Monogatari'', is a record of folk legends, stories, and traditions (as opposed to a {{ill|Folklore|en|Folklore|WD=Q36192|lt=folk tale}}) gathered in {{ill|Tōno, Iwate|en|Tōno, Iwate|WD=Q646766|lt=Tōno}}, a city in {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, Japan. Famous {{ill|Yōkai|en|Yōkai|WD=Q503186|lt=Yōkai}} in the stories include [[:en:Kappa (mythical creature)|kappa]] and {{ill|Zashiki-warashi|en|Zashiki-warashi|WD=Q148209|lt=Zashiki-warashi}}.{{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Ronald A. Morse|title=The Legends of Tono|publisher=Lexington Books|year=2008|isbn=978-0-7391-2767-4}} *''Kagyūkō'' (蝸牛考) – Yanagita revealed that the distribution of dialects for the word ''snail'' forms {{ill|Concentric circles|en|Concentric circles|WD=Q67251689|lt=Concentric circles}} on the Japanese archipelago ({{ill|Center versus periphery|en|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022|lt=Center versus periphery}} theory of dialectical diffusion over time). *''Momotarō no Tanjō'' (桃太郎の誕生) – In this work, Yanagita provides analysis into themes of Japanese folklore and society. The name of the work is derived from the famous Japanese tale of [[Momotarō]], as one of the examples he uses in his commentary on folktales as a form of reference material for understanding Japanese culture. In this work, he analyzes Momotarō to discuss some facets of Japanese society as a whole. His methodology in this has since been followed by many ethnologists and anthropologists. *''Kaijō no Michi'' (海上の道) – This piece, published only a year before Yanagita’s death, records the history, culture, and folk tradition of the [[Okinawa]] islands of Japan. In his studies of Okinawa, Yanagita sought the origins of Japanese culture in the area, though many of his speculations were denied by later researchers. It is also said that his inspiration for this research came from picking up a {{ill|Arecaceae|en|Arecaceae|WD=Q14080|lt=palm nut}} borne by the {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}} when he was wandering on a beach in the cape of {{ill|Cape Irago|en|Cape Irago|WD=Q371114|lt=Irago Misaki}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. *''Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale'' – This is a selection of Japanese folktales and data from Yanagita Kunio's ''Nihon mukashi-banashi meii'' (日本昔話名彙), translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer. {{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer|title=Yanagita Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale|publisher=Indiana University Press|year=1986|isbn=0-253-36812-X|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/en/publications/afm/afm9/}} *''Nochi no Kari-kotoba no Ki'' (後狩詞記) – He privately published a work based on his travels around [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]], focusing on the traditions and ways of life of a mountain community from {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, including details about their hunting practices and the vocabulary used to discuss it. This work is considered one of the first works of Japanese folklore studies from Japan. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Agriculture|Ancient Japan|Biography}} * {{ill|Center versus periphery|en|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022|lt=Center versus periphery}}: Yanagita's theory about {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect}}al diffusion and vocabulary propagation over time * {{ill|Nihonjinron|en|Nihonjinron|WD=Q1761112|lt=Nihonjinron}} * [[:ja:柳田國男・松岡家顕彰会記念館|Yanagita･Matsuoka Family Memorial (Japanese)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commonscat|Kunio Yanagita}} == External links == * {{Find a Grave|6136353}} {{Japanese folklore long}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[en:Kunio Yanagita]] [[arz:كونيو ياناجيتا]] [[bn:কুনিও ইয়ানাগিতা]] [[de:Yanagita Kunio]] [[eo:Yanagita Kunio]] [[es:Kunio Yanagita]] [[fa:کونیو یاناگیتا]] [[fr:Kunio Yanagita]] [[hu:Janagita Kunio]] [[id:Kunio Yanagita]] [[it:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ja:柳田國男]] [[ko:야나기타 구니오]] [[pl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[pt:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ru:Янагита, Кунио]] [[tl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[uk:Янаґіта Куніо]] [[wuu:柳田国男]] [[zh:柳田國男]] {{wikidata link|Q971687}} [[Category:1875 births]] [[Category:1962 deaths]] [[Category:Academic staff of Keio University]] [[Category:Japanese ethnologists]] [[Category:Japanese folklorists]] [[Category:Japanese government officials]] [[Category:Japanese lexicographers]] [[Category:Japanese nationalists]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of Culture]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:University of Tokyo alumni]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

He began his career as a bureaucrat, but developed an interest in rural Japan and its folk traditions. This led to a change in his career. His pursuit of this led to his eventual establishment of [[Japanese folklore|Japanese native folkloristics]], or ''minzokugaku'', as an academic field in Japan. As a result, he is often considered to be the father of modern Japanese folklore studies.{{cite book|last1=Mori|first1=Koichi|title=Yanagita Kunio: An Interpretive Study|date=1980|publisher=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|location=Nanzan University|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2210}} ==Early life== Yanagita was born as the fifth child of the Matsuoka family in the town of {{ill|Fukusaki, Hyōgo|en|Fukusaki, Hyōgo|WD=Q1207159|lt=Fukusaki}}, located in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. He was born with the name Kunio Matsuoka (or Matsuoka Kunio in the Japanese manner of naming), but was adopted into the family of a court justice named Naohei Yanagita. At the time, it was fairly common practice for families without a son {{ill|Japanese adult adoption|en|Japanese adult adoption|WD=Q17050042|lt=to adopt a young boy or man into the family}} to inherit the family’s property. This would often occur through marriage, with the adopting family marrying a daughter of the family off to their chosen heir as a way of binding him to the family. In this particular case, a match was made between the future folklorist and Naohei’s daughter, Taka. The two were wed in 1901, and his name was changed to Kunio Yanagita. Yanagita was known from a fairly young age for his interest in literature, particularly that of poetry. He also was a fan of Western literature. As he began to take an interest in folklore, Yanagita began reading ethnologies by Western anthropologists, such as {{ill|Edward Burnett Tylor|en|Edward Burnett Tylor|WD=Q141037|lt=Edward Burnett Tylor}}, shaping his later work.[[File:Matsuoka5brothers.JPG|thumb|200px|left|The Matsuoka brothers prior to Kunio's adoption by Yanagita]] ==Career== After graduating with a degree in law from [[:en:Tokyo Imperial University|Tokyo Imperial University]], Yanagita began a career as a civil servant, working for the Department of Agricultural Administration of the {{ill|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|en|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|WD=Q3315480|lt=Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce}}, that would last for about 20 years. Over the course of his time in bureaucracy, duties, Yanagita traveled around the countrysides of [[Honshu|Honshū]], the mainland of Japan. During these business trips, Yanagita became increasingly focused on the affairs of rural villages and their agricultural economic policy. As time passed, Yanagita began growing increasingly critical of the lack of concern for local autonomy allowed by the policies favored by his fellow civil servants. He gradually began to advocate in support of these groups, pushing for a shift in agricultural focus to center around cooperatives of small farmers rather than wealthy landlords. It is believed that the pushback he received against his values and ideas may have contributed to his change in careers and shift toward folklore studies.{{Cite journal|last=Oguma|first=Makoto|date=2015|title=The Study of Japan through Japanese Folklore Studies|journal=The Japanese Journal of Psychology|volume=16|issue=92|pages=236–237|doi=10.4992/jjpsy1912.16.236|issn=1884-1066|doi-access=free}} Yanagita’s departure from the Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce allowed him the opportunity to further investigate rural Japan. He began in-depth analysis, traveling around to record stories of local customs, practices, and beliefs. It was at this point that his literary friends, including writer [[:en:Shimazaki Toson|Shimazaki Toson]], began encouraging him to publish works based on oral traditions and customs of rural villages. His most famous example of this is a book known as ''The Legends of Tōno'' (1912). It is a compilation of short stories, practices, beliefs, and anecdotes from {{ill|Tōno, Iwate|en|Tōno, Iwate|WD=Q646766|lt=Tōno}}, a small, rural community surrounded by mountains in {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate}}. From here, Yanagita’s work developed into the anthropological study of folklore that he is still known for today. He published many other works, including several with folklorist {{ill|Kizen Sasaki|en|Kizen Sasaki|WD=Q2646063|lt=Kizen Sasaki}}, with whom he collaborated extensively. Yanagita's focus on local traditions was part of a larger effort to insert the lives of commoners into narratives of [[:en:Japanese history|Japanese history]].{{Cite journal|last=Morse|first=Ronald|date=1995|title=Untitled Review of "The Origins of Ethnography in Japan: Yanagita Kunio and His Times"|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2385561|journal=Monumenta Nipponica|volume=50|issue=3|pages=411–413|doi=10.2307/2385561|jstor=2385561|via=JSTOR}} He argued that historical narratives were typically dominated by events pertaining to rulers and high-Ranking officials. Yanagita claimed that these narratives focused on elite-centered historical events and ignored the relative uneventfulness and repetition that characterized the lives of ordinary Japanese people across history. He emphasized the unique practices of different groups of common people, such as ''sanka'' or mountain dwellers, and island dwellers. He also focused primarily on what he saw as the three areas of folklore studies: material objects, oral transmission, and mental or emotional phenomenon. This third Category, accessible only to those who share a deep understanding through similar experiences, is considered the main focus of folklore studies.{{Cite journal|last=Knecht|first=Peter|date=1992|title=Yanagita Kunio and the Folklore Movement: The Search for Japan's National Character and Distinctiveness|url=https://doi.org/10.2307/1178346|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=51|pages=353–355|doi=10.2307/1178346|jstor=1178346|via=JSTOR}} As a whole, Yanagita’s work is highly memorable and genre-defining. He is one of the premiere folklorists of Japan, and he helped to create the field of ''minzokugaku'' itself, earning him the title of “father of modern Japanese folklore.” == Major works == [[File:Kunio yanagita01 1920.jpg|thumb|250px|Yakusai Matsuoka's home]] *''Tōno Monogatari'' (遠野物語) – Yanagita’s most famous work, ''Tōno Monogatari'', is a record of folk legends, stories, and traditions (as opposed to a {{ill|Folklore|en|Folklore|WD=Q36192|lt=folk tale}}) gathered in {{ill|Tōno, Iwate|en|Tōno, Iwate|WD=Q646766|lt=Tōno}}, a city in {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, Japan. Famous {{ill|Yōkai|en|Yōkai|WD=Q503186|lt=Yōkai}} in the stories include [[:en:Kappa (mythical creature)|kappa]] and {{ill|Zashiki-warashi|en|Zashiki-warashi|WD=Q148209|lt=Zashiki-warashi}}.{{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Ronald A. Morse|title=The Legends of Tono|publisher=Lexington Books|year=2008|isbn=978-0-7391-2767-4}} *''Kagyūkō'' (蝸牛考) – Yanagita revealed that the distribution of dialects for the word ''snail'' forms {{ill|Concentric circles|en|Concentric circles|WD=Q67251689|lt=Concentric circles}} on the Japanese archipelago ({{ill|Center versus periphery|en|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022|lt=Center versus periphery}} theory of dialectical diffusion over time). *''Momotarō no Tanjō'' (桃太郎の誕生) – In this work, Yanagita provides analysis into themes of Japanese folklore and society. The name of the work is derived from the famous Japanese tale of [[Momotarō]], as one of the examples he uses in his commentary on folktales as a form of reference material for understanding Japanese culture. In this work, he analyzes Momotarō to discuss some facets of Japanese society as a whole. His methodology in this has since been followed by many ethnologists and anthropologists. *''Kaijō no Michi'' (海上の道) – This piece, published only a year before Yanagita’s death, records the history, culture, and folk tradition of the [[Okinawa]] islands of Japan. In his studies of Okinawa, Yanagita sought the origins of Japanese culture in the area, though many of his speculations were denied by later researchers. It is also said that his inspiration for this research came from picking up a {{ill|Arecaceae|en|Arecaceae|WD=Q14080|lt=palm nut}} borne by the {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}} when he was wandering on a beach in the cape of {{ill|Cape Irago|en|Cape Irago|WD=Q371114|lt=Irago Misaki}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. *''Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale'' – This is a selection of Japanese folktales and data from Yanagita Kunio's ''Nihon mukashi-banashi meii'' (日本昔話名彙), translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer. {{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer|title=Yanagita Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale|publisher=Indiana University Press|year=1986|isbn=0-253-36812-X|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/en/publications/afm/afm9/}} *''Nochi no Kari-kotoba no Ki'' (後狩詞記) – He privately published a work based on his travels around [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]], focusing on the traditions and ways of life of a mountain community from {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, including details about their hunting practices and the vocabulary used to discuss it. This work is considered one of the first works of Japanese folklore studies from Japan. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Agriculture|Ancient Japan|Biography}} * {{ill|Center versus periphery|en|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022|lt=Center versus periphery}}: Yanagita's theory about {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect}}al diffusion and vocabulary propagation over time * {{ill|Nihonjinron|en|Nihonjinron|WD=Q1761112|lt=Nihonjinron}} * [[:ja:柳田國男・松岡家顕彰会記念館|Yanagita･Matsuoka Family Memorial (Japanese)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commonscat|Kunio Yanagita}} == External links == * {{Find a Grave|6136353}} {{Japanese folklore long}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[en:Kunio Yanagita]] [[arz:كونيو ياناجيتا]] [[bn:কুনিও ইয়ানাগিতা]] [[de:Yanagita Kunio]] [[eo:Yanagita Kunio]] [[es:Kunio Yanagita]] [[fa:کونیو یاناگیتا]] [[fr:Kunio Yanagita]] [[hu:Janagita Kunio]] [[id:Kunio Yanagita]] [[it:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ja:柳田國男]] [[ko:야나기타 구니오]] [[pl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[pt:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ru:Янагита, Кунио]] [[tl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[uk:Янаґіта Куніо]] [[wuu:柳田国男]] [[zh:柳田國男]] {{wikidata link|Q971687}} [[Category:1875 births]] [[Category:1962 deaths]] [[Category:Academic staff of Keio University]] [[Category:Japanese ethnologists]] [[Category:Japanese folklorists]] [[Category:Japanese government officials]] [[Category:Japanese lexicographers]] [[Category:Japanese nationalists]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of Culture]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:University of Tokyo alumni]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

{{nobold|{{lang|ja|柳田 國男}}}} |image = File:Kunio Yanagita.jpg |image_size = 200px |caption = Kunio Yanagita, circa 1940 |birth_name = Kunio Matsuoka |birth_date = {{birth date|1875|7|31}} |birth_place = {{ill|Fukusaki|WD=Q1207159}} |death_date = {{death date|1962|8|8}} |death_place = {{ill|Tokyo|WD=Q7473516}} |known_for = Tōno Monogatari ({{lang|ja|遠野物語}})

''Nihon mukashibanashi meii'' ("Japanese Folk Tales") |occupation = {{ill|Esperantist|WD=Q860918}}; {{ill|agronomist|WD=Q1781198}}; {{ill|anthropologist|WD=Q4773904}}; {{ill|ethnologist|WD=Q1371378}}; {{ill|folklorist|WD=Q3075052}}; {{ill|lexicographer|WD=Q14972848}}; {{ill|linguist|WD=Q14467526}}; {{ill|poet|WD=Q49757}}; {{ill|university teacher|WD=Q1622272}}; {{ill|writer|WD=Q36180}} |nationality = {{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} |spouse = Taka Yanagita (1904) |parents = Yakusai Matsuoka (father) Naohei Yanagita (father-in-law) |module = {{Infobox Chinese | child=yes |kyujitai=栁田 國男 |shinjitai=柳田 国男 |romaji=Yanagita Kunio }} |gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} |title= {{ill|Shokikanchō|WD=Q11515193}} |father= {{ill|Misao Matsuoka|WD=Q11530013}} |relatives= {{ill|Eikyū Matsuoka|WD=Q11530016}}; {{ill|Inoue Michiyasu|WD=Q11373249}}; {{ill|Kanae Matsuoka|WD=Q11530083}}; {{ill|Shizuo Matsuoka|WD=Q6278269}} |awards= {{ill|Asahi Prize|WD=Q720434}}; {{ill|Order of Culture|WD=Q530124}}; {{ill|Order of the Rising Sun, 1st class|WD=Q10901470}}; {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|WD=Q2545449}} |notable_works= {{ill|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022}}; {{ill|Kagyū Kō|WD=Q11625819}}; {{ill|Tōno Monogatari|WD=Q10553521}} |native_name= 柳田国男; 柳田國男 |given_name= {{ill|Kunio|WD=Q1635977}} |resting_place= {{ill|Shunjū-en|WD=Q56349527}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kunio Yanagita'''|柳田 國男|Yanagita Kunio|lead=yes|extra=July 31, 1875 – August 8, 1962}} was a Japanese author, scholar, and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklorist}}. He began his career as a bureaucrat, but developed an interest in rural Japan and its folk traditions. This led to a change in his career. His pursuit of this led to his eventual establishment of [[Japanese folklore|Japanese native folkloristics]], or ''minzokugaku'', as an academic field in Japan. As a result, he is often considered to be the father of modern Japanese folklore studies.{{cite book|last1=Mori|first1=Koichi|title=Yanagita Kunio: An Interpretive Study|date=1980|publisher=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|location=Nanzan University|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2210}} ==Early life== Yanagita was born as the fifth child of the Matsuoka family in the town of {{ill|Fukusaki, Hyōgo|en|Fukusaki, Hyōgo|WD=Q1207159|lt=Fukusaki}}, located in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. He was born with the name Kunio Matsuoka (or Matsuoka Kunio in the Japanese manner of naming), but was adopted into the family of a court justice named Naohei Yanagita. At the time, it was fairly common practice for families without a son {{ill|Japanese adult adoption|en|Japanese adult adoption|WD=Q17050042|lt=to adopt a young boy or man into the family}} to inherit the family’s property. This would often occur through marriage, with the adopting family marrying a daughter of the family off to their chosen heir as a way of binding him to the family. In this particular case, a match was made between the future folklorist and Naohei’s daughter, Taka. The two were wed in 1901, and his name was changed to Kunio Yanagita. Yanagita was known from a fairly young age for his interest in literature, particularly that of poetry. He also was a fan of Western literature. As he began to take an interest in folklore, Yanagita began reading ethnologies by Western anthropologists, such as {{ill|Edward Burnett Tylor|en|Edward Burnett Tylor|WD=Q141037|lt=Edward Burnett Tylor}}, shaping his later work.[[File:Matsuoka5brothers.JPG|thumb|200px|left|The Matsuoka brothers prior to Kunio's adoption by Yanagita]] ==Career== After graduating with a degree in law from [[:en:Tokyo Imperial University|Tokyo Imperial University]], Yanagita began a career as a civil servant, working for the Department of Agricultural Administration of the {{ill|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|en|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|WD=Q3315480|lt=Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce}}, that would last for about 20 years. Over the course of his time in bureaucracy, duties, Yanagita traveled around the countrysides of [[Honshu|Honshū]], the mainland of Japan. During these business trips, Yanagita became increasingly focused on the affairs of rural villages and their agricultural economic policy. As time passed, Yanagita began growing increasingly critical of the lack of concern for local autonomy allowed by the policies favored by his fellow civil servants. He gradually began to advocate in support of these groups, pushing for a shift in agricultural focus to center around cooperatives of small farmers rather than wealthy landlords. From here, Yanagita’s work developed into the anthropological study of folklore that he is still known for today. Yanagita claimed that these narratives focused on elite-centered historical events and ignored the relative uneventfulness and repetition that characterized the lives of ordinary Japanese people across history. He emphasized the unique practices of different groups of common people, such as ''sanka'' or mountain dwellers, and island dwellers. This third Category, accessible only to those who share a deep understanding through similar experiences, is considered the main focus of folklore studies.{{Cite journal|last=Knecht|first=Peter|date=1992|title=Yanagita Kunio and the Folklore Movement: The Search for Japan's National Character and Distinctiveness|url=https://doi.org/10.2307/1178346|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=51|pages=353–355|doi=10.2307/1178346|jstor=1178346|via=JSTOR}} As a whole, Yanagita’s work is highly memorable and genre-defining. Morse|title=The Legends of Tono|publisher=Lexington Books|year=2008|isbn=978-0-7391-2767-4}} *''Kagyūkō'' (蝸牛考) – Yanagita revealed that the distribution of dialects for the word ''snail'' forms {{ill|Concentric circles|en|Concentric circles|WD=Q67251689|lt=Concentric circles}} on the Japanese archipelago ({{ill|Center versus periphery|en|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022|lt=Center versus periphery}} theory of dialectical diffusion over time). *''Momotarō no Tanjō'' (桃太郎の誕生) – In this work, Yanagita provides analysis into themes of Japanese folklore and society. In his studies of Okinawa, Yanagita sought the origins of Japanese culture in the area, though many of his speculations were denied by later researchers. It is also said that his inspiration for this research came from picking up a {{ill|Arecaceae|en|Arecaceae|WD=Q14080|lt=palm nut}} borne by the {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}} when he was wandering on a beach in the cape of {{ill|Cape Irago|en|Cape Irago|WD=Q371114|lt=Irago Misaki}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. *''Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale'' – This is a selection of Japanese folktales and data from Yanagita Kunio's ''Nihon mukashi-banashi meii'' (日本昔話名彙), translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer. {{cite book|first1=Kunio|last1=Yanagita|last2=Translated by Fanny Hagin Meyer|title=Yanagita Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale|publisher=Indiana University Press|year=1986|isbn=0-253-36812-X|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/en/publications/afm/afm9/}} *''Nochi no Kari-kotoba no Ki'' (後狩詞記) – He privately published a work based on his travels around [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]], focusing on the traditions and ways of life of a mountain community from {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, including details about their hunting practices and the vocabulary used to discuss it. This work is considered one of the first works of Japanese folklore studies from Japan. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Agriculture|Ancient Japan|Biography}} * {{ill|Center versus periphery|en|Center versus periphery|WD=Q5060022|lt=Center versus periphery}}: Yanagita's theory about {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect}}al diffusion and vocabulary propagation over time * {{ill|Nihonjinron|en|Nihonjinron|WD=Q1761112|lt=Nihonjinron}} * [[:ja:柳田國男・松岡家顕彰会記念館|Yanagita･Matsuoka Family Memorial (Japanese)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commonscat|Kunio Yanagita}} == External links == * {{Find a Grave|6136353}} {{Japanese folklore long}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[en:Kunio Yanagita]] [[arz:كونيو ياناجيتا]] [[bn:কুনিও ইয়ানাগিতা]] [[de:Yanagita Kunio]] [[eo:Yanagita Kunio]] [[es:Kunio Yanagita]] [[fa:کونیو یاناگیتا]] [[fr:Kunio Yanagita]] [[hu:Janagita Kunio]] [[id:Kunio Yanagita]] [[it:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ja:柳田國男]] [[ko:야나기타 구니오]] [[pl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[pt:Kunio Yanagita]] [[ru:Янагита, Кунио]] [[tl:Kunio Yanagita]] [[uk:Янаґіта Куніо]] [[wuu:柳田国男]] [[zh:柳田國男]] {{wikidata link|Q971687}} [[Category:1875 births]] [[Category:1962 deaths]] [[Category:Academic staff of Keio University]] [[Category:Japanese ethnologists]] [[Category:Japanese folklorists]] [[Category:Japanese government officials]] [[Category:Japanese lexicographers]] [[Category:Japanese nationalists]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of Culture]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:University of Tokyo alumni]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
